profile.behavior: Usually keeps low in grass, walking or running while foraging; males sing from low exposed stalks or fence wire. Flies short distances with rapid, fluttering wingbeats when flushed.
field_marks: Small, compact sparrow with a flat crown, thick neck, and very short tail.
Large, deep, conical bill for its size; bill is pale pinkish to horn-colored.
Plain buffy breast and white belly, typically without adult breast streaking.
Mottled brown, black, tan, and chestnut upperparts with pale-edged wing feathers.
Thin pale eye-ring and a distinct orange-yellow spot in front of the eye.
profile.habitat: Open grasslands, prairies, meadows, hayfields, and pastures, usually with sparse shrub cover and some bare ground.

sources.title: Grasshopper Sparrow Identification — All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology
Grasshopper Sparrow Life History — All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology

findings: Independent verification confirms Grasshopper Sparrow, Ammodramus savannarum, in Passerellidae.
All measurements printed on the plate match Cornell: length 4.3–4.5 in (10.8–11.5 cm), weight 0.5–0.7 oz (14–20 g), and wingspan 7.9 in (20 cm).

The plate accurately depicts and states the key adult marks: compact flat-crowned, thick-necked, short-tailed form; large pale conical bill; buffy unstreaked breast with white belly; mottled tan/black/chestnut upperparts; thin pale eye-ring and orange-yellow lore; and pale pink legs and feet. These agree with the supplied field photographs.
Habitat and behavior text agrees with the live Cornell and Audubon accounts. No place name, ZIP code, coordinates, map, or local-observation detail appears.
No concrete material anatomy, species, plumage, text, or composition issue found. The illustrated bird has one head, one beak, two wings, two legs, and one tail.
